performance_optimizations.title: analytics/monthly-trends uses full load_all() instead of analytics-optimised loader
performance_optimizations.description: The /api/v1/analytics/monthly-trends endpoint calls _get_all_cases() (31-column full load) instead of _get_analytics_cases() (7-column lightweight loader). Every other analytics endpoint uses the lightweight path. monthly-trends only needs date, outcome, and court_code — all available in the analytics loader — yet it loads all 31 fields for 149,016 cases. Additionally, the endpoint skips the _analytics_set() / _analytics_get() pre-computed result cache used by every other analytics endpoint, so the 149k-row Python loop runs on every request.
performance_optimizations.rationale: On Supabase free tier, load_all() takes ~57s vs ~13s for load_analytics_cases(). With no result cache the 149k-row aggregation loop also re-executes on every page visit. This is the single highest-impact backend fix.
performance_optimizations.currentMetric: ~57s Supabase load (full 31 cols) + uncached Python aggregation loop on every request
performance_optimizations.expectedImprovement: ~13s load (analytics 7 cols, 4x faster) + result served from 10-min cache on subsequent requests — effectively < 50ms after warm
performance_optimizations.implementation: 1. Replace `_get_all_cases()` with `_get_analytics_cases()` in analytics_monthly_trends. 2. Add cache key: `ck = 'monthly-trends:' + _analytics_cache_key()`. 3. Add early return if `_analytics_get(ck) is not None`. 4. Add `_analytics_set(ck, result)` before returning. 5. Verify date and outcome fields are included in the analytics column list in supabase_repository.py.
performance_optimizations.tradeoffs: Analytics cases loader omits full_text and some metadata fields — not needed for monthly aggregation. If a future version of monthly_trends needs additional columns, load_analytics_cases() would need updating.

plan.title: Switch to _get_analytics_cases()
plan.description: In analytics_monthly_trends (api.py:4147), change `_apply_filters(_get_all_cases())` to `_apply_filters(_get_analytics_cases())`

plan.title: Add result cache
plan.description: Before the aggregation loop, add: `ck = 'monthly-trends:' + _analytics_cache_key(); cached = _analytics_get(ck); if cached is not None: return _analytics_response(cached)`. After building `result`, add `_analytics_set(ck, result)` before the return.

plan.title: Verify analytics columns include date/outcome/court_code
plan.description: Confirm supabase_repository.py load_analytics_cases() selects date, outcome, court_code — these are the three fields consumed by this endpoint.

performance_optimizations.title: CasesPage renders up to 100 table rows with inline JSX on every state change
performance_optimizations.description: CasesPage renders up to 100 row `<tr>` elements directly inside the component body (api.py:885-992 table loop; line 1001-1009 cards loop). Each row contains badge components (CourtBadge, OutcomeBadge, NatureBadge), a formatDateCompact call, and string expressions. CaseCard is memo-wrapped but the table rows are raw JSX — not memoised at all. Any state change that touches the parent (e.g. selection checkbox toggle, keyboard focus, viewMode toggle) causes all 100 rows to re-evaluate their JSX and badge components even though most rows have not changed.
performance_optimizations.rationale: The table row `<tr>` is the hot render path — called 100 times per render. With 100 rows and multiple child components per row, a single checkbox toggle triggers O(100) child re-evaluations. Extracting rows to a memo component reduces this to O(1) changed row.
performance_optimizations.currentMetric: 100 un-memoised row evaluations per any state change in CasesPage
performance_optimizations.expectedImprovement: Only changed row(s) re-render on selection/focus changes; full list re-renders only when data or filters change
performance_optimizations.implementation: 1. Extract a CaseTableRow component from the inline `<tr>` JSX (lines 886-992). 2. Wrap with React.memo comparing case_id, selected state, and focusedIdx. 3. Pass only the required props: case object, isSelected boolean, isFocused boolean, onSelect callback, onNavigate callback. 4. Similarly extract CaseCardItem from the cards loop (line 1002-1009) if not already the memoised CaseCard. 5. Ensure all callbacks passed to rows use useCallback (toggleSelect already is — verify navigate is stable).
performance_optimizations.tradeoffs: Adds a small component extraction refactor. The Set<string> `selected` must not be passed directly as a prop (referential equality will always fail); instead pass a derived `isSelected: boolean` per row.

plan.title: Extract CaseTableRow memo component
plan.description: Create `CaseTableRow = memo(function CaseTableRow({ c, isSelected, isFocused, onSelect, onNavigate }: Props) { ... })` using the existing inline `<tr>` JSX. Derive `isSelected` and `isFocused` booleans before the map call.

plan.title: Update CasesPage render to use CaseTableRow
plan.description: Replace the inline `<tr>` JSX inside `cases.map()` with `<CaseTableRow key={c.case_id} c={c} isSelected={selected.has(c.case_id)} isFocused={clampedFocusedIdx === i} onSelect={toggleSelect} onNavigate={...} />`.

plan.title: Verify stable callbacks
plan.description: Confirm navigate from useNavigate is stable (it is in react-router-dom v7). Add useCallback wrapper for onNavigate if needed.

performance_optimizations.title: QueryClient has no gcTime configured — inactive queries evicted after default 5 minutes
performance_optimizations.description: App.tsx creates QueryClient with only `staleTime: 5 * 60 * 1000` in defaultOptions. The `gcTime` (garbage collection time, formerly cacheTime) is not set, leaving it at the TanStack Query v5 default of 5 minutes. For this app — where legal DB data is static within a session and analytics results are expensive to recompute — evicting cache entries after 5 minutes of inactivity means returning to a page like /analytics after navigating away triggers a full refetch from scratch even though the data is still fresh. Setting gcTime to 30+ minutes would keep the pre-warmed analytics responses in memory for the typical session duration.
performance_optimizations.rationale: Analytics endpoints load 149k-case aggregations that take 13-57s cold. A user who navigates Dashboard → Analytics → Cases → Analytics gets a full re-fetch on the second analytics visit if more than 5 minutes elapsed, even though staleTime is 5 min and the data is still valid. gcTime should exceed staleTime by a substantial margin.
performance_optimizations.currentMetric: gcTime = 300_000ms (default); inactive queries evicted 5 min after last use
performance_optimizations.expectedImprovement: Analytics cache survives typical session navigation (30-60 min); eliminates redundant re-fetches on page revisit within a session
performance_optimizations.implementation: 1. In App.tsx QueryClient constructor, add `gcTime: 30 * 60 * 1000` to defaultOptions.queries. 2. Optionally override per-hook for data that should age out faster (e.g. job status, pipeline state). 3. For analytics hooks that rarely change, consider increasing staleTime to 15 * 60_000 to match server-side 10-min analytics cache TTL.
performance_optimizations.tradeoffs: Higher memory usage if many distinct query keys are cached simultaneously. With ~15 analytics endpoints × ~10 filter combinations each, worst case is ~150 cached responses — acceptable for a legal research tool used in focused sessions.

plan.title: Add gcTime to QueryClient defaultOptions
plan.description: In App.tsx line 155-163, add `gcTime: 30 * 60 * 1000` inside `defaultOptions.queries`. This extends cache lifetime to 30 minutes.

plan.title: Increase analytics staleTime to match server cache TTL
plan.description: In use-analytics.ts, change `staleTime: 5 * 60_000` to `staleTime: 10 * 60_000` to align with the server-side 10-minute analytics cache TTL. This prevents unnecessary background refetches when the server would return the same cached result anyway.

plan.title: Override gcTime for live-data hooks
plan.description: For hooks like job status (use-stats.ts staleTime 30s) and pipeline state, add explicit `gcTime: 60_000` to prevent stale job data from being served from cache after inactivity.

performance_optimizations.title: DesignTokensPage is 2663 lines in a single module — loaded into the lazy chunk with no internal splitting
performance_optimizations.description: DesignTokensPage.tsx is 2663 lines. While it is correctly lazy-loaded, the entire file is parsed and executed when a user first visits /design-tokens. The page appears to contain large static token display tables, color swatches, and documentation sections that could be split into sub-sections. Additionally, it imports lucide-react icons (1 import statement across 63 files) — lucide-react v0.564.0 ships individual ESM files so tree-shaking should work correctly with Vite, but the sheer volume of icons imported across 63 files means any barrel-import style can degrade chunk quality.
performance_optimizations.rationale: A 2663-line file takes significantly longer to parse and evaluate at runtime than smaller modules, even if code-split at the route level. The DesignTokensPage is a developer/internal page not on the critical user path, so its parse cost should be deferred further with internal lazy sub-sections.
performance_optimizations.currentMetric: 2663 lines parsed/evaluated on first visit to /design-tokens route
performance_optimizations.expectedImprovement: Split into 3-4 lazy sub-sections (Colors, Typography, Spacing, Components); each section evaluates only when scrolled into view or tab-selected — reducing initial parse by ~60-70%
performance_optimizations.implementation: 1. Audit DesignTokensPage.tsx to identify major sections (Color Palette, Typography Scale, Spacing, Component tokens). 2. Extract each section into its own component file (e.g. TokenColorsSection.tsx, TokenTypographySection.tsx). 3. Lazy-load sections with React.lazy() + Suspense inside DesignTokensPage if it uses a tabbed/section UI. 4. Alternatively, split the page into sub-routes under /design-tokens/:section. 5. Verify lucide-react named imports are individual (e.g. `import { CheckCircle } from 'lucide-react'`) not namespace imports.
performance_optimizations.tradeoffs: Internal developer page — lower priority than user-facing optimisations. The effort to split a 2663-line file may not be worth it unless the page is actively used during development sessions.

plan.title: Audit DesignTokensPage section boundaries
plan.description: Read DesignTokensPage.tsx to identify the major section components/JSX blocks. Map line ranges to logical sections.

plan.title: Extract section components
plan.description: Move each major section (≥400 lines) into a dedicated file under frontend/src/components/design-tokens/. Keep section components non-lazy for now; simply reducing file size improves parse time and editor performance.

plan.title: Lazy-load heavy sections
plan.description: If DesignTokensPage uses tabs or accordion sections, wrap the non-visible sections in React.lazy() + Suspense so they are only evaluated when the user navigates to them.

performance_optimizations.title: No HTTP cache headers on paginated case list responses — every navigation refetches from server
performance_optimizations.description: The /api/v1/cases endpoint does not set Cache-Control response headers (unlike /analytics/* which correctly sets `public, max-age=600`). The TanStack Query staleTime for useCases is only 10_000ms (10 seconds). Users navigating between pages (Cases → Case Detail → back to Cases) trigger a fresh server round-trip after 10 seconds even though the underlying data has not changed. For a 149k-row legal database with infrequent writes, the case list is effectively static within a user session.
performance_optimizations.rationale: With staleTime=10s, a user browsing cases who looks at a case detail for 15 seconds and navigates back will always see a loading state before the list reappears. Increasing staleTime to 60s (matching the count query) and adding server-side Cache-Control headers allows CDN/browser caching and eliminates the majority of redundant API calls.
performance_optimizations.currentMetric: staleTime=10s for case list; no Cache-Control on /api/v1/cases responses; every navigation after 10s triggers network round-trip
performance_optimizations.expectedImprovement: With staleTime=60s and server Cache-Control max-age=60, users browsing cases see instant list restores for typical navigation patterns; reduces API calls by ~70% for browse-heavy sessions
performance_optimizations.implementation: 1. In use-cases.ts, increase staleTime from 10_000 to 60_000 (matching useCaseCount). 2. In api.py, find the cases list endpoint and add a _cases_list_response() helper that sets Cache-Control: public, max-age=60, stale-while-revalidate=30. 3. For write-heavy mutations (createCase, updateCase, deleteCase), the existing qc.invalidateQueries already handles cache busting correctly. 4. Optionally set Vary: Accept-Encoding on the response.
performance_optimizations.tradeoffs: 10-second staleTime was likely chosen to show newly created/edited cases promptly. Increasing to 60s means edits take up to 60s to reflect — acceptable since mutations already call invalidateQueries which bypasses staleTime entirely for that session.

plan.title: Increase useCases staleTime to 60s
plan.description: In frontend/src/hooks/use-cases.ts line 34, change `staleTime: 10_000` to `staleTime: 60_000`.

plan.title: Add Cache-Control to case list endpoint
plan.description: In api.py, find the /api/v1/cases endpoint handler. Add a response helper (like _stats_response) that sets Cache-Control: public, max-age=60, stale-while-revalidate=30 on case list responses.

plan.title: Verify mutation cache invalidation still works
plan.description: Run the existing test suite to confirm that useCreateCase/useUpdateCase/useDeleteCase onSuccess callbacks still invalidate the cache correctly and the updated list appears immediately after a write.

performance_optimizations.title: No virtualisation for the 100-row case table — all 100 rows are DOM nodes simultaneously
performance_optimizations.description: CasesPage renders all 100 cases (the full page_size) as DOM nodes at once in both table view (lines 884-994) and card view (lines 1001-1009). With 100 rows each containing ~8 cells and 3 badge sub-components, this produces ~800 DOM nodes per render. There is no virtual scrolling — rows outside the viewport are still in the DOM and contribute to layout and paint time. For the cards view (3 columns), all 100 cards including their full-text truncated descriptions are rendered simultaneously.
performance_optimizations.rationale: Modern browsers handle 100 DOM nodes reasonably, but each of the ~800 nodes in the table participates in layout. A 100-row table with wide columns (min-width: 1100px) forces a layout calculation across the full scrollable width. Virtualising to ~15-20 visible rows would reduce DOM node count by ~80% and eliminate layout of off-screen rows.
performance_optimizations.currentMetric: ~800 DOM nodes for 100-row table (8 cells × 100 rows); full layout calculated for all rows including off-screen
performance_optimizations.expectedImprovement: With virtualisation (~20 visible rows), reduce active DOM nodes to ~160; initial paint and scroll performance improved especially on low-end devices
performance_optimizations.implementation: 1. Install @tanstack/react-virtual (already a TanStack family dependency, small addition). 2. In CasesPage, replace the `cases.map()` inside `<tbody>` with a virtualised row renderer using useVirtualizer from @tanstack/react-virtual. 3. Set estimateSize to the measured row height (~40px). 4. Apply the virtualizer's totalSize to the container and offset each row with a translateY transform. 5. The table header stays fixed; only tbody is virtualised. 6. For card view, use a CSS grid with content-visibility: auto on each card as a lower-effort alternative.
performance_optimizations.tradeoffs: Table virtualisation requires fixed or estimated row heights. Variable-height rows (e.g. multi-line titles) need measured heights or a min-height estimate. The existing 100-row page_size is already small enough that this is a medium-priority optimisation rather than critical.

plan.title: Add @tanstack/react-virtual dependency
plan.description: Run `npm install @tanstack/react-virtual` in the frontend directory. This is a tiny (3kb) sibling package to the already-used @tanstack/react-query.

plan.title: Implement useVirtualizer in CasesPage table
plan.description: Wrap the tableRef container with useVirtualizer({ count: cases.length, getScrollElement: () => tableRef.current, estimateSize: () => 40 }). Replace `cases.map()` with `virtualizer.getVirtualItems().map()`, applying offsetTop to each row.

plan.title: Apply content-visibility to card view
plan.description: As a lower-effort alternative for the cards grid, add `content-visibility: auto; contain-intrinsic-size: 200px` CSS to each CaseCard container div. This allows the browser to skip rendering off-screen cards without a JS virtualiser.

performance_optimizations.title: lucide-react imported across 63 files without a shared icon barrel — may fragment tree-shaking in some bundler configurations
performance_optimizations.description: lucide-react is imported in 63 out of ~151 frontend files. Each file imports named icons directly (`import { Search, ChevronDown } from 'lucide-react'`). While Vite + Rollup should tree-shake these correctly via ESM named exports, the current manualChunks config in vite.config.ts does not include lucide-react in any chunk definition. This means Rollup will inline icon SVG code into whichever chunk first imports it, potentially splitting icon code across multiple lazy-loaded page chunks and duplicating SVG path data. Adding lucide-react to manualChunks as a shared 'icons' chunk ensures all icon code is deduplicated into a single chunk loaded once.
performance_optimizations.rationale: lucide-react v0.564.0 is a large package (~100+ icons used across 63 files). Without a dedicated chunk, Rollup may duplicate commonly used icons (Search, ChevronDown, etc.) across multiple code-split chunks. A shared icons chunk is downloaded once and cached.
performance_optimizations.currentMetric: lucide-react split across multiple lazy chunks; icon SVG code potentially duplicated; no shared icons chunk
performance_optimizations.expectedImprovement: Single icons chunk downloaded once and cached; eliminates icon code duplication across page chunks; reduces per-page-chunk size by the icon code that was duplicated
performance_optimizations.implementation: 1. In vite.config.ts manualChunks, add `icons: ['lucide-react']` alongside the existing vendor/query/charts/dnd entries. 2. Run `npm run build` and inspect the chunk output to confirm all lucide-react code is in the icons chunk. 3. Verify no icon code appears in other chunks using `npx rollup-plugin-visualizer` or `vite-bundle-visualizer`.
performance_optimizations.tradeoffs: Adding a shared icons chunk adds one more initial HTTP request for pages that use icons (all of them). However, since all pages use icons, the icons chunk is effectively part of the critical path and will be cached after first load — the deduplication benefit outweighs the extra request.

plan.title: Add icons chunk to vite.config.ts manualChunks
plan.description: In frontend/vite.config.ts lines 38-48, add `icons: ['lucide-react']` to the manualChunks object.

plan.title: Build and verify chunk output
plan.description: Run `cd frontend && npm run build` and check the dist output. Confirm a single icons-*.js chunk exists and that other page chunks do not contain lucide SVG paths.

plan.title: Optional: add i18next to shared chunks
plan.description: i18next + react-i18next are imported across many pages. Add `i18n: ['i18next', 'react-i18next', 'i18next-browser-languagedetector']` to manualChunks to similarly deduplicate the i18n runtime.

ui_ux_improvements.title: ConfirmModal missing ARIA dialog semantics and focus trap
ui_ux_improvements.description: ConfirmModal renders a visually correct modal but lacks role="dialog", aria-modal="true", aria-labelledby, and aria-describedby attributes. The backdrop click dismisses but keyboard focus is not trapped inside the modal — a user tabbing past the two buttons will reach content behind the overlay.
ui_ux_improvements.rationale: Screen-reader users cannot identify this as a dialog. Focus escape from modals is a WCAG 2.1 AA failure (criterion 2.1.2 No Keyboard Trap is violated in the opposite direction: focus leaves the modal unintentionally). This is the confirmation modal for destructive batch-delete actions, so accessibility here is high-stakes.
ui_ux_improvements.current_state: div.fixed.inset-0 wrapping two buttons. ESC closes via document listener. No role, aria-modal, aria-labelledby, or focus trap. Cancel button gets focus via useEffect but Tab will exit the modal.
ui_ux_improvements.proposed_change: 1) Add role="dialog" aria-modal="true" aria-labelledby="confirm-modal-title" aria-describedby="confirm-modal-desc" to the inner container div. 2) Add id="confirm-modal-title" to the h3 and id="confirm-modal-desc" to the p. 3) Add a focus-trap via useEffect that intercepts Tab/Shift+Tab and keeps focus cycling between the Cancel and Confirm buttons only while the modal is open.
ui_ux_improvements.user_benefit: Screen-reader users (including vision-impaired applicants) will hear the modal title announced on open, understand it is a dialog, and will not accidentally trigger actions behind the overlay.

plan.title: Add ARIA role and labelling attributes
plan.description: On the inner container div (the white card), add role="dialog" aria-modal="true" aria-labelledby="confirm-modal-title" aria-describedby="confirm-modal-desc". Add matching id attributes to the existing h3 and p elements.

plan.title: Implement keyboard focus trap
plan.description: In a useEffect that runs when open=true, collect all focusable elements inside the modal container ref. On keydown Tab, if focus is on the last element cycle to first; on Shift+Tab from first, cycle to last.

plan.title: Restore focus on close
plan.description: Capture document.activeElement before the modal opens (store in a ref) and restore focus to that element when the modal closes, so keyboard users return to where they were.

ui_ux_improvements.title: GlobalSearch dialog missing ARIA dialog role and live-region for results
ui_ux_improvements.description: The GlobalSearch overlay renders a visually functional command-palette style dialog but has no role="dialog", aria-modal, or aria-labelledby. The results list has no role="listbox" / role="option" pairing with the input's aria-activedescendant, so screen readers cannot announce the number of results or the currently highlighted item.
ui_ux_improvements.rationale: WCAG 2.1 AA — 4.1.3 Status Messages and 1.3.1 Info and Relationships. The search dialog is the primary discovery mechanism for immigration applicants; if a screen-reader user cannot determine how many results appeared or which is highlighted, it is effectively unusable.
ui_ux_improvements.current_state: Outer fixed div with backdrop. Inner div with input + ul of results. aria-current on result buttons but no combobox/listbox pattern. No aria-live region announces result count changes.
ui_ux_improvements.proposed_change: 1) Add role="dialog" aria-modal="true" aria-label to the inner container. 2) Change the input to role="combobox" aria-expanded={hasResults} aria-haspopup="listbox" aria-autocomplete="list" aria-controls="global-search-listbox" aria-activedescendant={activeResultId}. 3) Change the ul to role="listbox" id="global-search-listbox". 4) Change each result button to role="option" with a stable id (e.g. result-{case_id}) and aria-selected. 5) Add a visually hidden aria-live="polite" region announcing result count on change.
ui_ux_improvements.user_benefit: Screen-reader users will hear result counts announced as they type, hear the highlighted result on arrow-key navigation, and can select results with Enter — all without visual feedback.

plan.title: Convert input to combobox pattern
plan.description: Add role="combobox", aria-expanded, aria-haspopup="listbox", aria-controls, and aria-activedescendant to the input element. Assign stable IDs to each result option matching the activedescendant value.

plan.title: Convert results list to listbox
plan.description: Change the ul to role="listbox" with a matching id. Change each result li/button to role="option" with aria-selected={idx === clampedActiveIdx} and id="global-search-result-{case_id}".

plan.title: Add live region for result count
plan.description: Add a visually hidden div with aria-live="polite" aria-atomic="true" that renders "{n} results" when cases.length changes. Use a 150ms debounce to avoid excessive announcements while typing.

ui_ux_improvements.title: CasesPage table has no skeleton loading state — shows blank gap during data fetch
ui_ux_improvements.description: When CasesPage first loads or when filters change, the table area renders nothing until isLoading is false. There is no skeleton/placeholder row structure, so the page visually collapses and reflows when data arrives. The existing skeleton pattern is used in JudgeHero, ComparisonTable, and ChartCard but not in the main cases list which has 100k+ records and meaningful load time.
ui_ux_improvements.rationale: Visual continuity during loading reduces perceived latency and prevents layout shift — critical for anxious applicants who may interpret a blank table as an error. TanStack Query's keepPreviousData helps on filter changes, but first-load and hard refreshes still show a blank region.
ui_ux_improvements.current_state: isLoading guard renders PageLoader (full-page spinner with text). When loading resolves the table appears. No row-level skeleton. The filter bar and header render immediately but the content area is empty.
ui_ux_improvements.proposed_change: Replace the PageLoader branch for the table section with an inline skeleton: render 8-10 rows of animate-pulse placeholder divs that match the table's column structure (a wide title column placeholder, a narrow court badge placeholder, a date placeholder). Keep the filter bar interactive during load. Use the same animate-pulse pattern already established in ComparisonTable and JudgeHero.
ui_ux_improvements.user_benefit: Users see the table structure immediately and understand data is incoming. Eliminates layout shift. Matches the quality bar already set by the judge/analytics pages.

plan.title: Create CasesTableSkeleton component
plan.description: Add a small inline component (or named function) CasesTableSkeleton that renders a table with 10 rows. Each row has: a checkbox placeholder, a wide title bar (h-4 w-2/3 animate-pulse rounded bg-surface), a court badge placeholder (h-5 w-12), a date placeholder (h-4 w-20), and an outcome placeholder (h-5 w-16). Use the existing token colours.

plan.title: Replace PageLoader with skeleton in table region
plan.description: In the CasesPage render, where isLoading currently triggers PageLoader, instead render the table header + CasesTableSkeleton rows inline so the filter bar, page header, and column headers remain visible and stable.

plan.title: Verify keepPreviousData behaviour on filter change
plan.description: Confirm useCases passes placeholderData: keepPreviousData (TanStack Query v5 syntax). If not, add it so filter changes show stale data overlaid with a subtle isFetching opacity-50 on the table body rather than a full skeleton re-render.

ui_ux_improvements.title: Sidebar collapsed mode icon-only buttons have no visible tooltip on mobile — rely solely on title attribute
ui_ux_improvements.description: When the Sidebar is collapsed (w-16), nav items render only an icon with a title attribute for the label. The title attribute produces a native browser tooltip on hover (desktop), but on mobile touch devices title is never exposed, leaving icon-only nav items completely unlabelled for touch users.
ui_ux_improvements.rationale: The collapsed sidebar is the primary navigation state on tablet viewports. Touch users have no way to discover what the icon means. This affects the entire navigation — 11 nav items across 4 groups.
ui_ux_improvements.current_state: NavLink has title={label} for collapsed state. No aria-label override. No tooltip component. On mobile title tooltips are not triggered by tap.
ui_ux_improvements.proposed_change: When collapsed=true, add aria-label={label} to each NavLink (currently only title is set). Additionally, implement a lightweight CSS tooltip via a ::after pseudo-element or a small absolutely-positioned sibling span that appears on :focus-visible and :hover, showing the label text to the right of the icon. This requires no new dependency — use Tailwind group/peer utilities.
ui_ux_improvements.user_benefit: Touch users on tablets can long-press or tap the icon and still read the label in the tooltip. Keyboard users navigating the collapsed sidebar will hear the label from aria-label. Eliminates a complete navigation discovery failure on mobile.

plan.title: Add aria-label to collapsed NavLink
plan.description: In Sidebar.tsx, when collapsed=true the NavLink already has title={label}. Add aria-label={label} alongside it so screen readers and assistive technology can identify the link regardless of visual mode.

plan.title: Implement CSS tooltip for collapsed icons
plan.description: Wrap each NavLink icon in a relative container. Add a sibling span with the label text, positioned absolutely to the right of the icon (left-full ml-2), with opacity-0 invisible group-hover:opacity-100 group-focus-within:opacity-100 transition. Style as a small rounded bg-card border border-border shadow text-xs px-2 py-1 whitespace-nowrap pointer-events-none z-50.

plan.title: Test collapsed sidebar on 768px viewport
plan.description: Resize browser to 768px, navigate the collapsed sidebar with keyboard Tab, and confirm aria-label is announced. Hover/focus each icon and confirm the tooltip appears to the right without being clipped by the sidebar boundary (overflow-visible on the nav container may be needed).

ui_ux_improvements.title: CourtBadge full name exposed only via title — inaccessible to screen readers and invisible on mobile
ui_ux_improvements.description: CourtBadge uses a title attribute to show the full court name (e.g. 'Administrative Appeals Tribunal' for 'AATA') on hover. The title attribute is not announced by screen readers by default and is never shown on touch devices. For non-legal users (the primary audience), the 3-4 letter abbreviation alone is opaque.
ui_ux_improvements.rationale: The design brief states 'Trust Before Beauty' and 'court colour coding is core information design'. An applicant who does not know what FMCA or FedCFamC2G means cannot build trust in the data. The full court name should be persistently accessible.
ui_ux_improvements.current_state: span with title={fullName}. The abbreviation is the only visible text. No aria-label. No tooltip component. Mobile users never see the full name.
ui_ux_improvements.proposed_change: Add aria-label={fullName ?? court} to the span so screen readers announce the full name. For sighted users, replace the raw title with a CSS tooltip (same pattern as uiux-004) that appears on hover/focus. Optionally add a variant prop (size='lg') that renders both the abbreviation and a truncated full name inline — useful in case detail page headers where there is more space.
ui_ux_improvements.user_benefit: Screen reader users hear 'Administrative Appeals Tribunal' not 'AATA'. Touch users can long-press and see the tooltip. Non-legal applicants immediately understand which body decided their type of case.

plan.title: Add aria-label with full court name
plan.description: In CourtBadge.tsx, change the span to include aria-label={fullName ?? court}. This is a one-line change with zero visual impact and immediately fixes screen-reader accessibility.

plan.title: Wrap in relative container for hover tooltip
plan.description: Wrap the span in a relative inline-flex container. Add a sibling tooltip span (same pattern as uiux-004) showing fullName, positioned above (bottom-full mb-1) with opacity transitions. Keep the existing title attribute as a belt-and-suspenders fallback.

plan.title: Verify rendering at high badge density

ui_ux_improvements.title: Keyword search input in CasesPage commits only on blur or Enter — no real-time feedback indicator
ui_ux_improvements.description: The keyword search input uses onBlur + Enter to commit the search (URL param update), not onChange. This is a deliberate debounce pattern to avoid per-keystroke API calls, but users receive zero visual feedback that their typed query has not yet been submitted. There is no loading spinner on the input or any indication of the pending-but-not-committed state.
ui_ux_improvements.rationale: Non-technical users (immigration applicants) expect search boxes to work like Google — type and results update. When they type a query and move focus elsewhere (e.g. click a court filter), the search fires silently. If they type and immediately click a result expecting filtered results, nothing happens. The pattern is correct architecturally but needs UX affordance.

ui_ux_improvements.user_benefit: Users immediately understand when their query is pending vs. applied. The loading indicator confirms the search is running. Reduces confusion and support burden for non-technical primary users.

code_quality.title: 39 broad `except Exception` catches in api.py swallow errors silently
code_quality.description: api.py contains 39 `except Exception` clauses. Of these, many (lines 323, 357, 1312, 1676, 1805, 1836, 2003, 2035, 2066, 2115, 2175, 2435, 2505, 3103, 3193) log nothing or only log at WARNING without the exception details, making production debugging very difficult. Several are marked 'pragma: no cover' indicating they are never tested.
code_quality.rationale: Broad catches without logging the exception type/traceback turn transient bugs into silent failures. The Flask error model means unhandled exceptions become 500s with full traceback — which is often more useful than a swallowed exception returning a degraded result.
code_quality.currentState:
39 `except Exception` clauses. Many examples:
- Line 323: `except Exception: pass` (completely silent)
- Line 1312: `except Exception: return None` (silent)
- Line 2003/2035/2066: cache-fill fallbacks that swallow all errors
- Lines 3373/3433/3486/3524: RPC fallbacks only log at WARNING without exc_info=True
code_quality.proposedChange: 1) For every `except Exception: pass` or `except Exception: return X` with no log — add `logger.debug(..., exc_info=True)` at minimum. 2) For RPC fallbacks that are expected failures — keep WARNING but add `exc_info=True` so the traceback is captured at DEBUG level. 3) For cache-fill exceptions — re-raise after logging or use `logger.exception()`. Audit all 39 instances against this policy.
code_quality.bestPractice: Error handling: log detailed error context on server side (coding-style.md), never silently swallow errors

code_quality.title: 25% of Python functions across the project are missing return type hints
code_quality.description: Across 487 Python functions in the project, 125 (25%) have no return type annotation. In api.py specifically, 51 public route handler functions have no return type. The project uses Python 3.14 with full type-annotation support available.
code_quality.rationale: Missing return type hints prevent pyright/mypy from catching return-value misuse. Flask route handlers should all be annotated as `-> Response` or `-> tuple[Response, int]` to catch the common bug of accidentally returning a plain dict instead of jsonify(dict).
code_quality.currentState: 125 of 487 Python functions (25%) lack return type hints. All 51 public route handlers in api.py are missing return annotations (get_csrf_token, stats, list_cases, analytics_outcomes, etc.). Private helpers like _is_win, _round_rate, _split_judges do have type hints — the problem is concentrated in public-facing functions.
code_quality.proposedChange: Add return type annotations to all 51 route handlers using `from flask import Response`: route handlers return `Response`, handlers that return error tuples return `tuple[Response, int]`. Enable pyright in strict mode for the web/routes/ directory via a per-directory pyrightconfig. Fix all type errors surfaced.
code_quality.bestPractice: Type safety, Python 3.14 type annotation best practices

code_quality.title: frontend/src/lib/api.ts at 1291 lines — split typed API client by domain

code_quality.rationale: A 1291-line single file for all API types and calls creates wide import surface area. Any change to one domain type causes the entire file to be re-evaluated by TypeScript. Splitting by domain enables tree-shaking, improves build-time type checking, and makes it clear which types belong to which feature.
code_quality.currentState: 1291 lines with 79 exports. All API call functions and their return types live in a single file. The file has been incrementally extended for every new feature (legislations, LLM council, taxonomy, semantic search) without a split strategy.
code_quality.proposedChange:
Split into domain-scoped modules under frontend/src/lib/api/:
  core.ts — fetchWithCsrf, ApiError, shared types
  cases.ts — CaseFilters, ImmigrationCase, fetchCases, fetchCase, ...
  analytics.ts — AnalyticsOutcomes, fetchAnalyticsOutcomes, ...
  taxonomy.ts — VisaLookup, fetchTaxonomyJudges, ...
  search.ts — SearchResult, fetchSearch, fetchSemanticSearch
  llm-council.ts — LlmCouncilResponse, fetchLlmCouncil, ...
  legislations.ts — Legislation, fetchLegislations, ...
frontend/src/lib/api.ts becomes a re-export barrel.
code_quality.bestPractice: File size ≤800 lines, high cohesion modules

code_quality.title: ESLint config missing no-console and explicit-function-return-type rules
code_quality.description: The project's eslint.config.ts uses typescript-eslint recommended preset but does not add @typescript-eslint/explicit-function-return-type or no-console rules. The tsconfig.app.json is appropriately strict (strict: true, noUnusedLocals, noUnusedParameters) but the ESLint layer has no runtime-behavior rules beyond react-hooks.
code_quality.rationale: no-console prevents debug console.log statements leaking to production. explicit-function-return-type enforces type-safe function signatures in React components and hooks. Both are low-friction rules with high signal value for a codebase this size.
code_quality.currentState: eslint.config.ts uses: js.configs.recommended + tseslint.configs.recommended + reactHooks.configs.flat.recommended + reactRefresh.configs.vite. No custom rules object. TypeScript strict mode is enabled in tsconfig but ESLint does not enforce function return types or ban console usage.
code_quality.proposedChange:
Add a rules block to eslint.config.ts:
  'no-console': ['warn', { allow: ['warn', 'error'] }]
  '@typescript-eslint/explicit-function-return-type': ['warn', { allowExpressions: true, allowTypedFunctionExpressions: true }]
The allowExpressions option means inline arrow functions in JSX props are exempt — only module-level functions and React components require explicit returns.
code_quality.bestPractice: Linting as first defence against debug code leaks and missing types

documentation_gaps.title: OpenAPI / Swagger Specification for all 50+ API endpoints
documentation_gaps.description: The project exposes 50+ REST endpoints under /api/v1/* (confirmed by grepping @api_bp.route). None have a machine-readable OpenAPI spec. Most route handler functions lack docstrings describing parameters, query strings, response shape, or error codes — only a handful (court_lineage, stats_trends, data_dictionary) have inline docstrings.
documentation_gaps.rationale: Developers integrating against the API (e.g. building alternative frontends, writing integration tests, or extending the scraper pipeline) cannot discover endpoint contracts without reading ~5000 lines of api.py. An OpenAPI spec also enables auto-generated client SDKs, interactive Swagger UI, and contract testing.

documentation_gaps.proposedContent: 1) Add flask-smorest or apispec to generate OpenAPI 3.1 from route decorators + marshmallow schemas. 2) At minimum, add docstrings to the 15 highest-traffic endpoints (stats, cases list, search, analytics/outcomes, analytics/judges, analytics/judge-profile, analytics/success-rate, court-lineage, filter-options, export/csv, llm-council/run, taxonomy/guided-search, taxonomy/visa-lookup, cases/<id>, cases/<id>/similar). 3) Serve /api/v1/docs with Swagger UI. Each docstring should cover: HTTP method, path params, query params with types/defaults, response envelope shape, and error codes.

documentation_gaps.title: ImmigrationCase field-level documentation (all 31 fields)
documentation_gaps.description: The ImmigrationCase dataclass in models.py defines 31 fields with zero inline comments. A /api/v1/data-dictionary endpoint exists that presumably returns some metadata, but the dataclass itself has no per-field docstrings explaining data type, source (regex vs LLM vs scraper), fill rate, or legal meaning. Fields like is_represented, legal_test_applied, visa_outcome_reason, and legal_concepts are particularly opaque.
documentation_gaps.rationale: Domain knowledge is critical here: a developer adding a new scraper, a researcher writing an analysis script, or a contributor extending the LLM extraction pipeline all need to know what each field means, where it comes from, and what values to expect. The CLAUDE.md fill-rate table helps developers but is not co-located with the code.

A /api/v1/data-dictionary endpoint exists at line 5010 of api.py but its DATA_DICTIONARY_FIELDS constant was not confirmed to have descriptions.
documentation_gaps.proposedContent: Add a comment block above each field group in the dataclass, e.g.: # -- Core identification -- case_id (str): First 12 hex chars of SHA-256(citation|url|title). Never empty after ensure_id(). # -- Scraper-populated fields -- court (str): Full court name, e.g. 'Administrative Appeals Tribunal'. court_code (str): Short code, one of AATA|ARTA|FCA|FCCA|FedCFamC2G|HCA|RRTA|MRTA|FMCA. # -- Regex-extracted fields (fill rates vary) -- visa_subclass (str): Human-readable subclass label, e.g. 'Student (500)'. Fill ~91.6%. visa_subclass_number (str): Numeric subclass only, e.g. '500'. is_represented (str): 'Y'/'N'/'' — whether applicant had legal representation. Fill ~42.4%. legal_test_applied (str): Short label for the legal test cited, <=80 chars. Fill ~36.2%.

documentation_gaps.title: Australian court lineage & outcome taxonomy guide for end users
documentation_gaps.description: The application serves non-legal immigration applicants who do not understand: (a) why cases appear under FMCA, FCCA, or FedCFamC2G for the same type of matter; (b) what 'Affirmed', 'Remitted', 'Set Aside', 'Quashed' mean in practice; (c) that a 'win' in a tribunal (Remitted/Set Aside) is different from a win in a court (Allowed). This domain knowledge is embedded in api.py's _OUTCOME_MAP and TRIBUNAL_CODES constants but is never surfaced to users.
documentation_gaps.rationale: The primary user is a self-represented immigration applicant under stress. If they misread 'Affirmed' as a positive outcome (the visa was affirmed = granted) when it actually means the refusal was upheld, they will draw incorrect conclusions about case precedents. This is a trust and accuracy issue, not just a UX nicety.
documentation_gaps.currentDocumentation: The /court-lineage API endpoint returns lineage metadata. A CourtLineagePage exists in the frontend. The CLAUDE.md has a Court Lineage table for developers. No user-facing glossary exists for outcomes.
documentation_gaps.proposedContent: 1) An in-app 'Outcome Glossary' tooltip or help panel on the Cases list and Case Detail pages explaining each of the 10 normalised outcomes (Affirmed, Dismissed, Remitted, Set Aside, Allowed, Granted, Quashed, Refused, Cancelled, Withdrawn) with a plain-English sentence and whether it counts as applicant success. 2) On the CourtLineagePage, add a paragraph explaining the court merger timeline and why searching across FMCA+FCCA+FedCFamC2G is needed for complete lower-court history. 3) In the README Section 5 (Usage), add a subsection 'Understanding Outcomes' with the same glossary.

documentation_gaps.title: Developer guide: adding a new storage backend (Repository Protocol)
documentation_gaps.description: The project has three repository implementations (CSV, SQLite, Supabase) behind a CaseRepository Protocol. A developer wanting to add a fourth backend (e.g. DuckDB, MongoDB, or a remote REST store) has no guide. The pattern is non-trivial: 15 required methods, WAL mode, FTS5 specifics, thread-local connections for SQLite, and Supabase-specific pgvector calls.
documentation_gaps.rationale: This is the most architecturally significant extension point in the system. Without documentation, any new backend will miss thread-safety requirements or FTS integration, leading to subtle runtime bugs.
documentation_gaps.currentDocumentation: CLAUDE.md lists the four files and names the Protocol but gives no implementation contract beyond method names. The repository.py Protocol file presumably has method signatures but no implementation notes.
documentation_gaps.proposedContent: A 'Adding a Storage Backend' section in CLAUDE.md (or a docs/backends.md) covering: 1) All 15 Protocol methods with their expected contracts (return types, exception behaviour, pagination semantics). 2) Thread-safety requirements (the SQLite backend uses thread-local connections). 3) How to register a new backend with the --backend CLI flag in web.py. 4) FTS / full-text search integration (optional but needed for /search). 5) A minimal 30-line skeleton implementation. 6) Which unit tests to run to verify compliance (pytest tests/test_repository_*.py).

plan.title: Read repository.py Protocol and document all 15 method contracts
plan.description: For each method: signature, return type, semantics (e.g. does get_case raise or return None on missing?), and whether it is required or optional for the protocol.

plan.title: Document thread-safety and connection lifecycle patterns
plan.description: Explain sqlite_repository.py thread-local connection pattern. Document why CSV backend is not thread-safe for concurrent writes.

documentation_gaps.title: Design token documentation: naming conventions, theming contract, and build pipeline
documentation_gaps.description: frontend/src/tokens/tokens.json defines the complete design token system (colors, typography, spacing, shadows, court colors for 9 courts) with no accompanying documentation. The build pipeline (scripts/build-tokens.ts) transforms this JSON into CSS variables and TypeScript constants, but the naming contract, how to add a new token, and which tokens are theme-aware (light/dark) is undocumented.
documentation_gaps.rationale: The CLAUDE.md design context section explicitly states 'All spacing, shadows, border-radius MUST come from tokens.json, not magic numbers.' With 24 pages and a growing component library, a new frontend contributor will violate this constraint without knowing it. Court color tokens in particular have a legal/semantic meaning (each maps to a specific court) that is not obvious from hex values alone.
documentation_gaps.currentDocumentation: tokens.json contains color, typography, spacing, shadow, and radius keys. CLAUDE.md mentions the token system exists. No README or comment explains the JSON schema, the light/dark.* nesting convention, or the build pipeline invocation.
documentation_gaps.proposedContent: A comment header block in tokens.json (or a companion tokens.md) covering: 1) Token naming convention: color.primary.DEFAULT, color.dark.primary.DEFAULT for the dark-mode override. 2) Court color semantic meaning: each of the 9 court codes (AATA, ARTA, FCA, FCCA, FedCFamC2G, HCA, RRTA, MRTA, FMCA) maps to a specific tribunal/court — the colors must NOT be changed without verifying all court badge components. 3) Build pipeline: run 'cd frontend && npm run build:tokens' to regenerate CSS and TS outputs. 4) How to add a new token: add to tokens.json, run build:tokens, import the generated TS constant. 5) Which tokens map to which CSS custom properties (--color-primary, --color-accent, etc.).

documentation_gaps.title: Troubleshooting guide: AustLII scraping failures, IP blocks, and rate limit recovery
documentation_gaps.description: AustLII is the primary data source and has multiple documented failure modes: HTTP 410 on default User-Agent, IP blocks from bulk scraping (resolves in hours), AATA 2025-2026 returning 500 (use ARTA instead), Federal Court DNS failure (search2.fedcourt.gov.au), and RRTA/MRTA anonymised case titles. These are mentioned briefly in CLAUDE.md's Gotchas section but there is no structured troubleshooting guide with diagnostic steps.
documentation_gaps.rationale: When a scrape fails silently (returns 0 cases instead of an error), operators waste hours diagnosing. These failure modes are reproducible and each has a known fix. A structured runbook prevents repeated diagnosis cycles. Particularly important for the Cloudflare Workers deployment path where log access is limited.
documentation_gaps.currentDocumentation: CLAUDE.md Gotchas section has 8 one-liner bullets. No structured diagnostic flow. No mention of how to verify an IP block has cleared vs. a configuration error.
documentation_gaps.proposedContent: A 'Troubleshooting Scraping Failures' section with a decision tree: 1) 0 cases returned for a court/year — check: is the court code correct? documentation_gaps.title: Frontend CSRF + authentication pattern documentation for API consumers
documentation_gaps.description: The api.ts file implements a CSRF token fetch-and-cache mechanism, per-method automatic token injection, AbortController signal chaining, per-endpoint timeout constants, and localStorage dashboard caching. None of this is documented. A developer adding a new hook or a third-party consumer calling the API directly will not know they need to: (1) GET /api/v1/csrf-token first, (2) include X-CSRFToken on all non-GET requests, (3) expect 429 rate-limit responses on POST endpoints.
documentation_gaps.rationale: CSRF misconfiguration is a security gap. Any new POST/PUT/DELETE frontend call that omits the token will silently fail with a 400 CSRF error that looks like a generic API failure. The apiFetch wrapper is the correct pattern but is not documented, so contributors duplicate ad-hoc fetch calls instead of using it.
documentation_gaps.currentDocumentation: CLAUDE.md mentions 'CSRF protection via flask-wtf; /api/v1/csrf-token endpoint for React SPA' in one sentence. api.ts has no JSDoc. security.py is not examined but presumably has CSRF configuration.
documentation_gaps.proposedContent: JSDoc comments on the key exported functions in api.ts: apiFetch (explain CSRF injection, timeout, abort signal chaining), fetchCsrfToken (lazy cache, when it is invalidated), and each timeout constant (why ANALYTICS_HEAVY_TIMEOUT_MS is 20s vs FILTER_OPTIONS_TIMEOUT_MS is 8s). Also add a section to CLAUDE.md under 'React Frontend Gotchas': 'All non-GET API calls must go through apiFetch() from lib/api.ts — never use raw fetch() for mutation calls, as CSRF token injection is not automatic otherwise. The token is cached in module scope and reset on 403 responses.'

documentation_gaps.title: SmartPipeline phase documentation: crawl strategies, auto-rotate logic, and checkpoint recovery

The module-level docstring covers the high-level intent but the strategy rotation logic, what triggers a strategy switch, and how to resume a failed pipeline run are not documented.
documentation_gaps.rationale: The pipeline is the most operationally complex component: it runs as a background daemon thread, emits structured events, and can be interrupted. security_hardening.title: Unauthenticated /api/v1/debug endpoint leaks infra internals
security_hardening.description: The /api/v1/debug endpoint (api.py:1856) is publicly accessible with no authentication, no rate limiting, and no environment guard. It returns: (1) the full /etc/resolv.conf and last 10 lines of /etc/hosts, (2) first 10 chars of SECRET_KEY, SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY, SUPABASE_ANON_KEY, HYPERDRIVE_DATABASE_URL, and APP_ENV, (3) a live HTTP probe to Supabase REST using the service role key, and (4) a full Python traceback on DB failure. The in-code comment says 'temporary — remove after debugging' but the route is still live in production.
security_hardening.rationale: Even truncated secrets (10 chars) can be used to narrow brute-force attacks. DNS/hosts exposure reveals cloud infra topology. Full tracebacks expose library versions and internal paths. The service role key used in the live HTTP probe is logged in Supabase audit logs whenever /debug is called, creating an audit trail linkage.
security_hardening.vulnerability: CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor
security_hardening.currentRisk: Any internet user can call GET /api/v1/debug on the production URL (https://immi.trackit.today/api/v1/debug) to enumerate: partial SECRET_KEY value, partial Supabase service role key, partial Hyperdrive DB URL, container DNS config, and live database connection status with Python tracebacks on failure.
security_hardening.remediation: Remove the /api/v1/debug route entirely (the comment already marks it temporary). If debugging capability is needed, gate it behind: (1) APP_ENV != 'production' check at the top of the function, and (2) an internal-only IP allowlist or shared secret header check. Never return env var values — even partial ones — in any response.

security_hardening.title: X-Hyperdrive-Url header trusted from any HTTP client — potential DB connection hijack
security_hardening.description: web/__init__.py lines 113-118 read the X-Hyperdrive-Url header from every incoming request and store it in flask.g without any validation, origin check, or secret verification. The comment says it is 'injected by the Cloudflare Worker proxy', but Flask receives HTTP directly and cannot distinguish a request from the legitimate Worker from a request crafted by an attacker. If any code path later uses g.hyperdrive_url to open a psycopg2 connection, an attacker can supply an arbitrary PostgreSQL connection string pointing to their own server to exfiltrate queries or inject credentials.
security_hardening.rationale: The HyperdriveRepository is instantiated at app startup from the env var HYPERDRIVE_DATABASE_URL, so currently g.hyperdrive_url is captured but not used for new connections at request time. However the pattern is dangerous: the hook silently persists attacker-supplied data into the request context with no validation. Future refactoring could inadvertently start using it, and the current code is one line away from being exploitable.
security_hardening.vulnerability: CWE-441: Unintended Proxy or Intermediary ('Confused Deputy')
security_hardening.currentRisk: If g.hyperdrive_url is ever used to open a DB connection, an attacker can supply an arbitrary PostgreSQL DSN via the X-Hyperdrive-Url header, potentially redirecting DB connections to an attacker-controlled server. Currently the value is stored but not used for new connections, reducing immediate exploitability — but the code path is one refactor away from being fully exploitable.
security_hardening.remediation: Either: (a) Remove the _capture_hyperdrive_url before_request hook entirely and read HYPERDRIVE_DATABASE_URL exclusively from os.environ at startup, or (b) If the header is genuinely needed for per-request routing, validate it against a HMAC-SHA256 shared secret set in both the Worker (as a Cloudflare Secret) and the Flask app (as an env var) before trusting it.

plan.title: Audit all usages of g.hyperdrive_url
plan.description: grep -rn 'g.hyperdrive_url' in the entire codebase to confirm no current request-time DB connection is opened with it.

plan.title: Remove or sign the header
plan.description: If g.hyperdrive_url is unused: delete the _capture_hyperdrive_url hook. If it is needed: add HMAC-SHA256 verification using a WORKER_INTERNAL_SECRET env var shared between the CF Worker and Flask.

plan.title: Add test for header rejection

security_hardening.title: Missing Permissions-Policy and HSTS headers; unsafe-inline in CSP
security_hardening.description: security.py lines 152-166 set five security headers but are missing: (1) Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) — required on any TLS-terminated service; without it, a first-visit HTTP downgrade is possible even when Cloudflare enforces HTTPS at the edge. (2) Permissions-Policy — browsers will grant camera, microphone, geolocation, and payment APIs by default without an explicit denial. (3) The Content-Security-Policy includes 'unsafe-inline' for both script-src and style-src, which allows any inline script or style to execute, defeating XSS protection for the React SPA. X-XSS-Protection is also absent (deprecated but still set by defense-in-depth best practice for older browsers).
security_hardening.rationale: The CSP unsafe-inline in script-src is the most significant gap: it means a stored or reflected XSS payload in any case text field that reaches the DOM will execute even with CSP enabled. The React SPA uses no dangerouslySetInnerHTML, reducing immediate XSS surface, but the header policy does not enforce this at the transport layer.
security_hardening.currentRisk: Without HSTS, first-visit HTTP requests on non-Cloudflare paths are not protected. Without Permissions-Policy, browser APIs are unrestricted. CSP unsafe-inline means a future XSS injection would not be blocked by the policy. Without a nonce or hash-based CSP, inline scripts can execute.
security_hardening.remediation: In add_security_headers(): (1) Add Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload. (2) Add Permissions-Policy: camera=(), microphone=(), geolocation=(), payment=(). (3) Remove 'unsafe-inline' from script-src and replace with a per-request nonce injected into Flask's response and the React index.html, or use a hash-based CSP. For style-src, unsafe-inline is often unavoidable with Tailwind; document it as accepted risk.

plan.title: Add HSTS and Permissions-Policy headers
plan.description: In add_security_headers() in security.py, add Strict-Transport-Security and Permissions-Policy headers after the existing ones. Gate HSTS on secure_cookie_required() to avoid breaking local dev.

plan.title: Replace CSP unsafe-inline with nonce
plan.description: Generate a per-request nonce in security_headers(), store in g.csp_nonce, and replace 'unsafe-inline' with 'nonce-{nonce}' in script-src. Update Flask templates to inject the nonce attribute on script tags.

security_hardening.title: FTS5 full-text search query double-quote escaping is insufficient — FTS5 operator injection
security_hardening.description: sqlite_repository.py lines 523-524 sanitize the FTS5 search query by wrapping in double-quotes and escaping internal double-quotes (replace('"', '""')). This prevents basic SQL injection, but FTS5 has its own query language. The double-quote wrapping forces phrase search, but FTS5 phrase queries can still be escaped by a user who supplies a query ending in a double-quote. For example, the input 'visa" OR title:* --' becomes the FTS5 expression '"visa"" OR title:* --"' which SQLite parses as the phrase 'visa"' followed by operator tokens. On SQLite 3.44+ the parser is stricter, but on older embedded versions FTS5 operator injection (NEAR, OR, NOT, column:) can cause unexpected result sets, DoS via extremely expensive queries (e.g. prefix match '*'), or errors that leak the internal query structure.
security_hardening.rationale: The immediate risk is performance-based DoS: a query like '"a* OR b*' after escaping becomes a broad prefix scan across the FTS5 index, potentially scanning the entire 149,016-case corpus. This exhausts SQLite I/O for the duration of the request. On older SQLite builds, the operational error is caught and silently ignored, returning an empty result, but the CPU/IO spike still occurs.
security_hardening.vulnerability: CWE-89: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command
security_hardening.currentRisk: An attacker can craft a search query that triggers expensive FTS5 prefix/wildcard scans, causing per-request CPU/IO spikes. On some SQLite versions, FTS5 operator syntax may partially leak through the escaping, causing unexpected query behavior.
security_hardening.remediation: Strip or reject FTS5 special characters before issuing the query. Safe approach: strip all characters except alphanumeric, spaces, and hyphens before wrapping in double-quotes. Alternatively, do not use the FTS5 MATCH operator for user-supplied queries — instead use parameterized LIKE on indexed columns, accepting slightly lower relevance ranking. Add a max-length check (e.g., 200 chars) before the query reaches SQLite.

plan.title: Reproduce FTS5 operator injection
plan.description: Write a pytest test that calls search_text() with 'visa" OR case_id:*' and confirms it returns a controlled result set without triggering an OperationalError or full-table scan.

plan.title: Sanitize FTS5 query input
plan.description: In sqlite_repository.py search_text(), add: safe_query = re.sub(r'[^\w\s\-]', ' ', query).strip() before the double-quote wrapping. Also add: if len(query) > 200: return []. This prevents FTS5 operator injection and limits query size.

security_hardening.title: LLM Council /run endpoint lacks authentication — arbitrary LLM queries billed to owner's API keys
security_hardening.description: POST /api/v1/llm-council/run (api.py:4827) is publicly accessible. It accepts a 'question' and 'context' body, calls three external LLM providers (OpenAI, Gemini, Anthropic) using the application owner's API keys from environment variables, and returns synthesized legal analysis. Rate limiting is applied (5 req/60s per IP), but IP-based rate limiting is trivially bypassed via distributed requests, proxies, or IPv6 rotation. There is no user authentication, no API key header requirement, and no cost attribution.
security_hardening.rationale: Each call to /llm-council/run triggers parallel API calls to up to 3 LLM providers. At current pricing (gpt-5.4 ~$15/1M output tokens, claude-sonnet-4-6 ~$15/1M output tokens), a 1000-request campaign across IPv6 addresses could generate $30-$50+ in API charges within minutes. The 5/60s rate limit per IP provides minimal protection against distributed abuse.
security_hardening.vulnerability: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function
security_hardening.currentRisk: Any internet user can generate unlimited LLM API charges against the application owner's OpenAI, Gemini, and Anthropic accounts by calling POST /api/v1/llm-council/run from distributed IPs or IPv6 addresses. The rate limiter (5/60s per IP) is the only protection.
security_hardening.remediation: Add one or more layers of protection: (1) Require a CSRF token for the POST request (CSRF protection is configured but its application to this endpoint should be verified). (2) Add a global per-day request budget check against a shared counter (Redis or SQLite). (3) Require a user-supplied application token header (even a simple shared secret in localStorage or a sign-up flow). (4) Increase rate limit aggressiveness: 3/60s per IP, 20/hour per IP, and a global 500/day cap. (5) Consider proxying LLM calls through a Cloudflare Worker with spend alerts.

security_hardening.title: Applicant names (PII) stored in localStorage bookmarks without encryption or expiry
security_hardening.description: use-bookmarks.ts stores bookmark entries in localStorage under the key 'immi-bookmarks'. Each BookmarkEntry includes case_id, citation, title, court_code, date, outcome — and the title field routinely contains applicant full names (e.g. 'Smith and Minister for Immigration'). The use-saved-searches.ts similarly stores CaseFilters (including applicant_name filter values) under 'saved-searches'. This data: (1) persists indefinitely with no TTL, (2) is accessible to any JavaScript running on the same origin (including injected ads or XSS payloads), (3) is never cleared on session end, (4) is transmitted in clear text in browser sync if the user has Chrome/Safari sync enabled.
security_hardening.rationale: Australian immigration case applicants are real people in sensitive legal proceedings. Persistent storage of their names in an unencrypted, unexipring localStorage key violates the Privacy Act 1988 (Cth) and the Australian Privacy Principles (APP 11 — Security of Personal Information). If an XSS vulnerability is ever introduced, localStorage contents including applicant names would be immediately exfiltrated.
security_hardening.vulnerability: CWE-312: Cleartext Storage of Sensitive Information
security_hardening.currentRisk: Applicant names from Australian immigration proceedings are stored indefinitely in browser localStorage. Any XSS attack would immediately exfiltrate this data. Data also persists after the user closes the browser, potentially exposing names on shared or public computers.
security_hardening.remediation: (1) Implement TTL for localStorage entries: wrap reads/writes with a timestamp and expire entries older than 30 days. (2) Avoid storing applicant_name in bookmark titles — store only case_id and citation, and fetch the full title on demand. (3) Add a 'Clear local data' button in the UI settings. (4) Consider sessionStorage instead of localStorage for saved searches (clears on tab close). (5) Document the data retention behaviour in a privacy notice.

security_hardening.title: In-process rate limiter bypassed by multi-worker / multi-container deployments
security_hardening.description: security.py lines 59-97 implement InMemoryRateLimiter — a per-process sliding window limiter stored in a Python dict. The Cloudflare Durable Object ('flask-v13') hosts a single Flask process, so within a single container this is functionally correct. However: (1) If the container is ever scaled horizontally (multiple instances), each instance has its own limiter state and the effective rate limit multiplies by the number of instances. (2) The limiter resets on every container restart (cold start). (3) For the LLM council endpoint (5/60s) this means a restart clears all hit counters, and a client timing requests around container idle-timeout cycling can exceed the limit. (4) TRUST_PROXY_HEADERS defaults to False, so all requests from the Cloudflare proxy appear to come from the same IP (the container-internal IP), causing all requests to share a single rate limit bucket.
security_hardening.rationale: The TRUST_PROXY_HEADERS=false default means request.remote_addr is the Worker's internal IP, not the client's real IP. This causes all users to share one rate limit bucket — a single aggressive user can trigger rate limiting for all users, and the bucket resets when the container restarts.
security_hardening.vulnerability: CWE-770: Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling
security_hardening.currentRisk: With TRUST_PROXY_HEADERS=false (default), all clients behind the Cloudflare proxy share one rate limit bucket keyed by the proxy's internal IP. One user hitting the limit blocks all users. Conversely, a client can restart the container to reset rate limit counters.
security_hardening.remediation: (1) Set TRUST_PROXY_HEADERS=true in production (the Worker always forwards the real client IP via X-Forwarded-For). (2) Validate that X-Forwarded-For is only trusted from the Cloudflare IP ranges (validate in the rate limit key function, not app-wide). (3) For the LLM council endpoint, supplement in-process rate limiting with a Cloudflare Worker rate limit rule at the edge (CF Rate Limiting product) so it persists across container restarts.

security_hardening.title: Export endpoints load all cases into memory without authentication — potential DoS + mass data exfiltration
security_hardening.description: GET /api/v1/export/csv and /api/v1/export/json (api.py:3120-3153) call repo.load_all() which loads all matched cases from the database, then slices to MAX_EXPORT_ROWS (50,000). These endpoints: (1) require no authentication, (2) have no rate limiting applied, (3) load up to 50,000 full case records (all 31 fields including applicant_name, country_of_origin, visa_subclass, hearing_date) into Python memory per request, (4) stream the result as a downloadable file. A single request to /api/v1/export/csv with no filters triggers a full 149,016-row database scan and serializes 50,000 rows of PII-containing case data. Multiple concurrent requests will exhaust container memory.
security_hardening.rationale: This combines a DoS vector (memory exhaustion from concurrent bulk exports) with a mass data exfiltration path (50,000 records of applicant names, nationalities, and visa details per unauthenticated request). The existing known issue #5 (unauthenticated destructive endpoints) is separate from this read-path concern.
security_hardening.vulnerability: CWE-359: Exposure of Private Personal Information to an Unauthorized Actor
security_hardening.currentRisk: Any internet user can download up to 50,000 immigration case records including applicant full names, countries of origin, visa subclasses, and hearing dates via a single unauthenticated HTTP GET request. Concurrent requests can exhaust container memory.
security_hardening.remediation: (1) Add @rate_limit(2, 60, scope='export') to both export endpoints. (2) Add a lower default MAX_EXPORT_ROWS cap (e.g., 5,000) unless a privileged token is provided. (3) Require a CSRF token or session cookie for export endpoints. (4) Consider whether export endpoints should be disabled in production entirely, given the public nature of the data; if kept, add logging of all export requests with client IP.
